title: SigLIP 2: Multilingual Vision-Language Encoders with Improved Semantic Understanding, Localization, and Dense Features
summary: We introduce SigLIP 2, a family of new multilingual vision-language encoders that build on the success of the original SigLIP. In this second iteration, we extend the original image-text training objective with several prior, independently developed techniques into a unified recipe -- this includes captioning-based pretraining, self-supervised losses (self-distillation, masked prediction) and online data curation. With these changes, SigLIP 2 models outperform their SigLIP counterparts at all mo

title: Twitter @bclavie
summary:
Interestingly, there doesn't seem to be any "killer input". Mapping the overlap of degraded inputs across models and the # of models any given question tris up paint a pretty heterogeneous picture.

This seems to suggest that this is truly about model capability itself. https://t.co/ItDLl2ieeE

title: Twitter @bclavie
summary:
Now the picture isn't actually so bleak! We've had VLMs for barely a year, and we can already see that while degradation is present, it's mild in lots of cases.
This benchmark isn't a herald of doom, it's just a useful measurement to understand where reading capabilities stand!

title: Twitter @bclavie
summary:
Bonus: it's often said that VLMs can do very well on blurry input, and if you're like me, your reference is usually @giffmana's blog post mentioning this. 

We generated screenshots across various resolutions, and it genuinely doesn't matter as long as it still looks like text. https://t.co/N3WnqX6deo

title: Twitter @bclavie
summary:
The results are pretty straightforward: here is degradation.
More importantly, most models do well on short-context, 1-page inputs. They don't do so well when there's more than 1 page of context involved.
This is problematic, because that's 99% of visual RAG scenarios! https://t.co/nTMC5SgjbY

title: Twitter @bclavie
summary:
We call this benchmark ReadBench. It's a very self-contained thing whose only aim is to measure the performance degradation between purely textual inputs and screenshots-of-text as inputs. 

To mimic real-world RAG scenarios, questions and instructions are kept as text.

title: nanoVLM
summary:
The simplest, fastest repository for training/finetuning small-sized VLMs. 
 !nanoVLMassetsnanoVLM.png nanoVLM is the simplest repository for trainingfinetuning a small sized Vision-Language Model with a lightweight implementation in pure PyTorch. title: maestro
summary:
streamline the fine-tuning process for multimodal models: PaliGemma, Florence-2, and Qwen2-VL 
 maestro VLM fine-tuning for everyone maestro is a streamlined tool to accelerate the fine-tuning of multimodal models. By encapsulating best practices from our core modules, maestro handles configuration, data loading, reproducibility, and training loop setup. It currently offers ready-to-use recipes for popular vision-language models such as Florence-2, PaliGemma 2, and
